stem: Based on the passage, which of the following can most reasonably be inferred about the original location of the tomato plants?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The soil drainage was poor due to the shade from the oak tree.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The oak tree was diseased and affecting the nearby plants.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tomato plants were not receiving any direct sunlight.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The northeast corner had the worst soil quality in the entire garden.

explanation: The passage provides several clues that support the inference that poor soil drainage was caused by the shade. The text states that the soil in the northeast corner 'was often damp and cool to the touch, even on hot days' and that this area 'was shaded by a large oak tree for most of the afternoon.' The experienced gardeners explained that 'tomatoes prefer well-drained soil that warms up during the day.' The logical connection is that the shade prevented the soil from warming up and drying out properly, leading to poor drainage conditions.

distractorRationale.A: This is the correct answer - the shade prevented soil from warming and drying, causing drainage problems.
distractorRationale.B: The passage never suggests the oak tree is diseased; it simply mentions that the tree creates shade.
distractorRationale.C: The passage explicitly states the area 'received morning sunlight,' so the plants did get some direct sunlight, just not enough.
distractorRationale.D: The passage only discusses the northeast corner's conditions and doesn't compare soil quality across the entire garden.

passage: When Maria first started working at the community garden, she noticed that the tomato plants in the northeast corner consistently produced smaller, paler fruit than those in other areas. The northeast section received morning sunlight but was shaded by a large oak tree for most of the afternoon. Maria observed that the soil in this area felt different too—it was often damp and cool to the touch, even on hot days. After consulting with experienced gardeners, she learned that tomatoes need at least six hours of direct sunlight daily to develop their full color and size. The gardeners also mentioned that tomatoes prefer well-drained soil that warms up during the day. Maria decided to relocate the tomato plants to the garden's southern border, where they would receive sunlight throughout the day and the soil dried out more completely between waterings.
